note_data.title: Statistics
keyPoints: **Data collection** methods include surveys, questionnaires, observations and experiments to gather primary data
**Frequency tables** organise raw data by counting how many times each value occurs
**Scatter diagrams** show the relationship between two variables with points plotted on x-y axes
**Mean** is the sum of all values divided by the number of values (for ungrouped data)
**Median** is the middle value when data is arranged in ascending order
**Mode** is the value that occurs most frequently in a data set
**Grouped data** uses class intervals when dealing with large ranges of continuous data
**Estimated mean** for grouped data uses midpoint of each class interval multiplied by frequency
**Modal class** is the class interval with the highest frequency in grouped data
**Correlation** describes the relationship shown in scatter diagrams as positive, negative or no correlation

definitions.definition: Data collected directly by the person conducting the investigation
The number of times a particular value or class interval occurs
A range of values used to group continuous data
A measure of how closely two variables are related
A value that lies outside the general pattern of the data

howToAnswer.instruction: Show clear working for mean/median/mode with correct formula substitution
Use midpoints for grouped data and state this is an estimate
State the type and strength of correlation from scatter diagram
Plot points accurately and label axes with units

modelAnswer.question:
The table shows the heights of students in a class in Karachi. Calculate an estimate of the mean height.

Height (cm) | 150-154 | 155-159 | 160-164 | 165-169
Frequency   |    3    |    7    |    5    |    2
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Correct sum and total frequency

commonMistakes: Never use class boundaries instead of midpoints for estimated mean calculations
Students often forget to arrange data in order before finding the median
Never say 'highest bar' for mode - always say 'highest frequency'
Students confuse modal class with actual mode in grouped data
Never draw correlation lines beyond the plotted data points
examinerTips: Statistics appears in every Paper 2 - usually worth 15-20 marks total
Always show midpoint calculations clearly for grouped data
Label axes and plot points accurately on scatter diagrams
State clearly whether answers are estimates for grouped data
formulas: Mean = Sum of values ÷ Number of values
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Scatter diagram with clearly labelled x and y axes, accurately plotted points, and line of best fit if correlation exists. Include title and units on both axes.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q5 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate mean, median, and mode from a frequency table and interpret which measure of central tendency was most appropriate
N/D 2022 P1 Q8 (4 marks) tested cumulative frequency curves where candidates had to draw the curve and find quartiles and interquartile range
M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) combined histogram construction with probability, asking students to complete a grouped frequency table and calculate probability of randomly selected values
N/D 2023 P1 Q7 (5 marks) focused on scatter diagrams and correlation, requiring students to plot points and describe the type and strength of correlation

workedExample.question: The table shows the masses of 50 apples. Complete the cumulative frequency table and draw a cumulative frequency curve. Use your graph to find the median and interquartile range. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Complete cumulative frequency by adding frequencies progressively: 0-30g (3), 0-40g (3+8=11), 0-50g (11+15=26), 0-60g (26+18=44), 0-70g (44+6=50)
Step 2: Plot cumulative frequency against upper class boundaries (30, 40, 50, 60, 70) with smooth curve through points
Step 3: Find median at n/2 = 50/2 = 25th value. Read across from 25 on y-axis to curve, then down to x-axis = 49g
Step 4: Find Q1 at 50/4 = 12.5th value = 42g, and Q3 at 3×50/4 = 37.5th value = 56g
workedExample.answer: Cumulative frequency table completed, smooth S-shaped curve drawn through plotted points, median = 49g, IQR = 14g
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for each correct cumulative frequency, 2 marks for correct curve, 1 mark each for median and quartiles read correctly, 1 mark for IQR calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the mean of grouped data formula and used midpoints incorrectly' — always use (lower boundary + upper boundary) ÷ 2 to find midpoints, not the class labels

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently plotted cumulative frequency against class midpoints instead of upper boundaries' — always plot cumulative frequency against the upper class boundary for accurate curve shape

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates lost marks by not showing working when estimating from graphs' — always draw clear horizontal and vertical lines on your graph and state the coordinates you're reading
note_data.keyDiagram: Cumulative frequency curve (ogive): Plot cumulative frequency on y-axis against upper class boundaries on x-axis. Draw smooth S-shaped curve through points (never join with straight lines). Include horizontal lines from n/4, n/2, 3n/4 positions on y-axis to curve, then vertical lines down to x-axis to read Q1, median, Q3. Label axes clearly with units and mark scale intervals evenly.
note_data.quickRecap: Statistics involves collecting, organizing and interpreting data using measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and spread (range, IQR). For grouped data, use midpoints and estimated values. Cumulative frequency curves help find quartiles - plot against upper boundaries and draw smooth curves. Always show working clearly and include units in final answers.
